mnemonics.title: Static vs Dynamic Scheduling
mnemonics.content: S.T.A.T.I.C. = Schedule To Assign Threads In Compile-time. D.Y.N.A.M.I.C. = Dynamically Yielding Needful Assignments Most Intuitively.

mnemonics.title: When to Use Dynamic Scheduling
mnemonics.content: V.A.R.I.A.B.L.E. = Variable execution times, Asymmetric workloads, Real-time processing, Imbalanced iterations, Bad memory access patterns, Complex dependencies, Uneven data sizes.

mnemonics.title: Scheduling Hierarchy
mnemonics.content: Static has LOWEST overhead, Dynamic has HIGHEST balance. Think: S-curve (Static = Small overhead, Static = Straight), D-curve (Dynamic = Demanding, Dynamic = Different chunks).

acronyms.expansion: OpenMP Environment Variable that controls runtime scheduling policy
acronyms.usage: export OMP_SCHEDULE="dynamic,50" sets chunk size to 50 with dynamic scheduling

acronyms.expansion: Single Instruction Multiple Data - scheduling affects vectorization potential
acronyms.usage: Static scheduling often enables better SIMD optimization by keeping iterations contiguous

acronyms.expansion: Non-Uniform Memory Access - scheduling affects data locality
acronyms.usage: In NUMA systems, static scheduling with proper affinity can significantly improve performance

visualTips.visualization: Think of a pizza being sliced beforehand - each person (thread) gets predetermined slices (chunks) before eating begins (runtime). No decision-making during eating.
Think of a restaurant buffet where people take one plate at a time. When finished, they get another plate. Each person gets immediate new work when available.

Think of a team dividing work: first, large tasks are assigned (efficient), but as work diminishes, smaller tasks ensure everyone stays busy (balanced). Like eating a pizza where you start with big slices but finish with smaller ones.
Imagine carrying boxes. Small boxes (chunk=1) = many trips but balanced load. Large boxes = few trips but one person might carry more weight. Optimal is somewhere in between.

analogies.analogy: Like assigning chapters of a book to students before reading - predetermined, no coordination needed during reading.
Like a study group where members grab the next problem when done - adaptive but requires coordination.
Like a marathon race where runners start in large groups but eventually spread out as they fatigue - initial efficiency, final balance.
Like a factory assembly line. Static = assign all tasks at morning meeting (low overhead). Dynamic = workers ask for next task every few minutes (high overhead, but adapts to delays).
